text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

James Gray gets a sneak preview of the apartments that will house nearly 15,000 athletes competing at this summer's Olympic Games

PARIS — AsOlympicathletes start arriving in Paris forthe biggest sporting event on earth, they may be concerned to discover their rooms do not have air-conditioning.

Average July and August highs in the French capital reach around 26°C, but heatwaves can send the mercury north of 40, as it has done twice in the last five summers.

The Olympic Villagewill be home to nearly 15,000 athletes, a unique community that the temporary residents often report as one of the best experiences of any Games – but extreme heat could turn that into a nightmare.

Instead of conventional air-conditioning units, each apartment is fitted with a water-based cooling system in the walls, which can lower the room temperature by as much as 10°C, while authorities will rely on the design of the buildings and advice to those on higher floors to keep blinds and windows closed during the day to assist with cooling.

“A brand new geothermal station behind the temporary transport mall will bring hot temperature [water] during the winter for the whole area,” says Laurent Michaud, the director of the Olympic & Paralympic Village, explaining that a 70-metre well provides water for the climate control system.

“The water is around 14°C. It’s actually pumped and injected into some stations into the ground floors of the buildings, and then it goes into the apartments.”

As many as 300Team GBathletes and performance staff will stay in some of the village’s 2,807 apartments at any one time during the Games, where they may also be surprised to find thermostats that only raise or lower the temperature by two degrees, rather than setting the temperature independently.

“The thermostat can reduce or accelerate the water in the pipe system, but the biggest setting is made in the stations at the ground floor of the buildings,” Michaud explains.

The communal areas of the apartment buildings will have conventional air-conditioning.

Organisers also believe that the street layout design will promote cooler air from the River Seine up the wide streets to the residential areas, as will the 9,000 trees and shrubs planted around the site. In 2003, 15,000 died in France during a heatwave, attributed in part to the lack of shade in the city which drove temperatures higher. Even now, only around 10 per cent of Paris is taken up by green spaces, compared to 33 per cent in London.

iwas given access to one of the apartments that will house athletes and some support staff, with a peak of around 300 Team GB athletes staying there at any one time during the Games.

Conditions on the day were not swelteringly hot, so it was hard to ascertain whether the new air-cooling systems really did work as officials insist they will.

The apartments are hardly palatial, with athletes sharing rooms and sleeping in single beds, which are bedecked with Paris 2024-branded duvets, which Olympians and Paralympians can take home as a souvenir. Otherwise, these “homes from home” are deliberately sparse.

The return of cardboard anti-sex beds, no air-con and space age anti-pollution machines – welcome to the Olympic Village As the Olympics gets underway i’s James Gray takes a sneak peak inside the newly built village that will host 15,000 of the worlds best athletes.#fyp#foryou#fy#foryoupage#olympic#olympics#paris#parisolympics2024#olympics2024#france🇫🇷#france#athletic

And then of course there are the famous cardboard beds, a flatpack marvel. Rumours swirled, as they did in Tokyo, that cardboard beds would be deployed that would collapse at any sudden movements, an attempt to discourage romantic endeavours.

They were dismissed, at both Games, as “fake news” andi’s platonic testing backed up suggestions that the beds (built to support 240kg) will withstand even the most vigorous activities – and frankly the suggestion that the planet’s fastest, strongest and most flexible people are not capable of having sex somewhere other than a bed is more than a little absurd.

Instead, the cardboard constructions, which when made up feel perfectly comfortable, are sustainable and recyclable solution to furnishing flats that will need to be emptied before new residents move in after the Games.

The phrase “Olympic village” is a misnomer of sorts, conjuring images of a sporting Glastonbury of tents, teepees and gazebos, whereas in reality, this is a brand new housing estate on the outskirts of central Paris.

The colour scheme is overwhelmingly beige, from the pavements made of seashells to the caramel apartment blocks that rise up to 10 storeys (but none above 20 metres) high over Saint-Ouen-sur-Seine, the post-industrial quarter a stone’s throw from the Stade de France and just over four miles from the Eiffel Tower.

In the 1940s and 1950s, all the electricity for Paris’s historic Metro system was generated in a plan which, now disused and repurposed, forms part of the village and will become an office block after the Games.

The village is bordered on one side by the Seine – in fact, part of the village has been built on an island in the river, complete with a new pedestrian bridge across from “mainland Paris”.

On another side, the eco-friendly neighbourhood is hemmed in by the busy A86, Paris’s “Super-peripherique” secondary ring road, but organisers insist there is mitigation its potential impact. Large acoustic walls have been erected to protect residents from noise, and a huge machine, that looks like a space age mushroom, is being deployed to filter out the more dangerous and pollutive particles from the air.

That is an innovation though rather than a tried-and-tested piece of machinery, one of a number of experimental schemes that building company Solideo, the public sector organisation in charge of building the Olympic facilities, hopes will succeed and become a more widespread legacy of the Games.

Legacy is an increasingly important part of Games hosting, because of its proximity to sustainability.

When the Olympics and Paralympics are over, there will be some adjustments required – for a start, every dwelling will need a kitchen added – but the hope is that the village will become a self-contained neighbourhood with 6,000 new residents, the same number of new employees in retail and office buildings, two new schools, a wide shopping boulevard and 15 acres of communal green space.

It is a worthy idyll of social mobility, giving a previously dangerous and undesirably part of town a welcome boost, but French media report that house prices in the village have already reached more than €7,000 (£6,000) per square metre for a three-bedroom apartment. For comparison, London’s average is just over £7,000 per square metre.

Paris 2024 organisers have promised that 25 per cent of the 2,807 apartments post-Games will be reserved as social housing – although in Seine-Saint-Denis, the widerdepartmentwhere the village is situated, more than 50 per cent of residents currently live in social housing.

The explanation offered by the mayor of Saint-Denis Mathieu Hanotin is that the village was not built as a “social housing village” but as “a place with a lot of solidarity”.

Hanotin added: “But for that to work, there must be people who earn money living alongside people who earn a little less. Otherwise, it’s not solidarity, it’s social separatism.”

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: Inside the Paris 2024 Olympic Village - where 300 Team GB stars are staying
author: James Gray
description: James Gray gets a sneak preview of the apartments that will house nearly 15,000 athletes competing at this summer's Olympic Games
